competitor_patterns: Extreme depth and comprehensiveness: Bikerumor's #1-ranking guide is 24,358 words — testing 18+ products with structured H2s per product, comparison tables, and FAQs. Our longest post is 2,690 words.

Frequent freshness signals: Bikerumor updated their guide just 5 days ago with named additions (Smith Syncline, Roka San Remo Air). Top competitors visibly timestamp updates to signal recency to both Google and readers.

Product-named H2 headers: Every top-ranking roundup uses individual product names as H2s (e.g. 'Oakley Sutro Lite', 'POC Devour', 'Tifosi Rail'), which captures long-tail product-specific searches and improves topical authority.

Comparison tables are standard: At least 2 of the top 3 competitors use structured HTML tables comparing weight, lens type, price, UV rating — a format Google parses for Featured Snippets and AI Overviews.

Trust signals embedded in content: Top competitors explicitly state how many products they tested ('We tested 18'), include methodology sections ('How do we test and why trust us?'), and feature editorial review language — building E-E-A-T signals our posts currently lack.
our_gaps: Word count is 10–20x below top competitors: Our best post is 2,690 words vs Bikerumor's 24,358. For competitive head terms like 'best cycling glasses 2026', we cannot rank without dramatically deeper content covering criteria, comparisons, and real-world test scenarios.

No comparison tables: None of our three recent posts include a table. Competitors use tables to win Featured Snippets and Google AI Overviews. For specs-heavy searches (weight, UV, anti-fog), tables are the expected format.

No named competitor comparisons in content: Our posts position StradaPro in isolation. Top-ranking pages name Oakley, Tifosi, POC, Smith — capturing searchers already considering those brands. We miss all 'StradaPro vs Oakley'-type intent.

No editorial methodology or trust section: We have no 'How we tested' or 'Why trust Velluto' section. Google's E-E-A-T guidelines reward demonstrated expertise. Our posts read as product promotion rather than authoritative editorial.

Thin post diversity — no seasonal or use-case segmentation: Competitors cover 'best cheap cycling glasses', 'road cycling glasses', 'triathlon glasses', 'gravel glasses' as distinct pages. We have one generic best-of post, missing the long-tail segmentation that drives cumulative traffic.
keyword_opportunities: interchangeable lens cycling glasses — Evil Eye ranks #1 for this and it maps directly to StradaPro's core click-in lens system. We have zero content targeting this exact phrase.
lightweight cycling sunglasses under 30g — a highly specific, low-competition query that StradaPro (25g) can own definitively. No current competitor content hits this weight-specific angle.

best cycling glasses for road cycling 2026 — a segmented variant of the head term where 'road cycling' specificity narrows competition. Our Amstel Gold Race post touches this but doesn't target the phrase directly.

anti-fog cycling glasses — StradaPro's built-in anti-fog system is a key differentiator, yet we have no dedicated content for this high-intent query. Competitors mention anti-fog as a feature bullet but none have a dedicated page.

cycling glasses 30-day trial — a transactional, near-zero-competition query that matches our unique risk-reversal offer. No competitor offers this; it's an owned keyword opportunity with high conversion intent.
next_post_guidelines: Write at minimum 4,000 words for any post targeting head terms ('best cycling glasses', 'road cycling glasses review'). Structure content with one H2 per sub-topic or product comparison, and include a minimum of one HTML comparison table per post.

Include a named product comparison section in every buying-guide post: explicitly compare StradaPro against at least 3 named competitors (Oakley Sutro Lite, Tifosi Rail, POC Devour) using a table with columns for weight, lens system, anti-fog, UV rating, trial policy, and price.

Add a visible 'How We Tested' or 'Why Trust This Review' section near the top of every guide-style post. Mention specific real-world test conditions (climbs, wet descents, early morning rides) to build E-E-A-T signals.

Target one specific long-tail keyword variant per post and place it in the H1, first paragraph, and at least two H2s. Do not try to rank for multiple head terms in one post — segmentation wins (e.g. dedicate a post solely to 'anti-fog cycling glasses' or 'lightweight cycling sunglasses under 30g').

Add a freshness update note at the top of every post ('Last updated: [date]') and commit to re-dating guides monthly. This directly mirrors the update signals Bikerumor uses to maintain #1 rankings across seasonal peaks.

Every post must include at minimum one FAQ schema block with 4–6 questions targeting 'People Also Ask' queries related to the post topic. Questions should match natural language searches like 'Are 25g cycling glasses worth it?' or 'Do anti-fog cycling glasses actually work?'

Embed the 30-day trial offer as a contextual CTA within the body of every post (not just a banner), framed as a direct competitive differentiator: 'Unlike Oakley or POC, Velluto lets you test on real rides for 30 days — no commitment.' This captures conversion intent from comparison-stage readers.
seo_quick_wins: Add an HTML comparison table to every existing and future buying-guide post, at minimum comparing StradaPro specs against 3 named competitors. Tables are the single fastest structural change to win Featured Snippets and Google AI Overview inclusions for spec-query searches.

Retroactively add a 'Last Updated' timestamp and a one-paragraph update note to all three existing posts — this costs 10 minutes per post and immediately improves freshness signals that Google uses to rank cycling gear guides, which have strong seasonal recency weighting.

Add internal links between all three existing posts using keyword-rich anchor text (e.g. link from the Amstel Gold Race post to the Best Cycling Glasses 2026 post using the anchor 'best road cycling glasses for spring classics') — currently the posts appear to be isolated, losing PageRank flow and topical cluster signals.
meta_title_fixes: No GSC data available yet — meta title fixes cannot be prioritised by CTR. Recommendation: install GSC tracking immediately and return to this analysis after 28 days of impression data. In the interim, apply best-practice title structures: lead with the target keyword, include the year, keep under 60 characters, and end with a differentiator (e.g. 'Best Cycling Glasses 2026 — Tested & Reviewed | Velluto').
content_quick_wins: No GSC data available yet — near-top keyword positions cannot be identified. Recommendation: prioritise creating content for 'interchangeable lens cycling glasses' and 'anti-fog cycling glasses' immediately, as these map to StradaPro's unique features and show clear competitor ranking gaps. Re-evaluate position-based quick wins after first 28-day GSC period.
geo_angles: Lead with verifiable, specific facts that AI systems can cite confidently: '25g frame weight', 'UV400 certified', '30-day risk-free trial', 'tool-free click-in lens change'. AI Overview and Perplexity prefer content with precise, attributable claims over generic marketing language. Structure these as scannable bullet specs near the top of product pages and blog posts.

Create a dedicated 'Velluto StradaPro vs [Competitor]' comparison hub page that AI crawlers can use to answer 'What is lighter than Oakley Sutro Lite?' or 'Which cycling glasses have a trial period?' — questions that AI systems increasingly answer by pulling structured comparison data from brand pages.

Publish a standalone 'Why 25 Grams Matters for Cycling Glasses' educational article with no direct product push in the first 600 words. AI systems (especially Perplexity and ChatGPT) preferentially cite educational, non-promotional content when answering 'what weight should cycling glasses be?' type queries — then reference StradaPro as the example later in the piece.
comparison_post_angles: COMPETITOR Oakley (Sutro Lite): angle — 'Oakley Sutro Lite vs Velluto StradaPro: Is €250 Worth It?' | Headline idea: 'Oakley Sutro Lite vs StradaPro: Weight, Lenses & Value Compared (2026)' | Key differentiator: Sutro Lite is heavier and uses a proprietary lens system with no trial period. Lead with: 'StradaPro weighs 25g and includes two lens systems plus a 30-day trial — here's how it compares to Oakley's best-seller at over twice the price.'

COMPETITOR Tifosi (Rail): angle — 'Tifosi Rail vs Velluto StradaPro: Budget Price vs Premium Performance?' | Headline idea: 'Tifosi Rail vs Velluto StradaPro: Which Cycling Glasses Win on Value in 2026?' | Key differentiator: Tifosi Rail is budget-positioned but lacks Italian design, adjustable nose fit, and a trial policy. Lead with: 'Both are mid-range priced — but only one lets you test on real climbs for 30 days before committing.'

COMPETITOR Evil Eye (interchangeable lens): angle — 'Evil Eye vs Velluto StradaPro: Interchangeable Lens Cycling Glasses Head-to-Head' | Headline idea: 'Best Interchangeable Lens Cycling Glasses 2026: Evil Eye vs Velluto StradaPro' | Key differentiator: Evil Eye ranks #1 for 'interchangeable lens cycling glasses' — this post directly attacks that keyword while showcasing StradaPro's click-in VellutoPuro/VellutoVisione system, lighter weight, and 30-day trial as the deciding advantages.
